metric_definitions.definition: Measures whether the generated email includes all required key facts accurately and naturally.

rubric.description: The email ignores the required facts or contradicts them.
Only a tiny fragment of one required fact appears.
Some facts are present, but multiple important facts are missing or distorted.
Most facts are present, but one important fact is missing, weakened, or ambiguous.
Almost all required facts are present, with only a minor omission or awkward compression.
Every required fact is included accurately and naturally, with no unsupported factual claims.

metric_definitions.definition: Measures whether the generated email matches the requested tone in context.

rubric.description: The tone is absent, opposite of the requested tone, or unusable for the context.
The tone is very far from the requested tone.
The tone only loosely resembles the requested tone and is noticeably mismatched.
The tone is partly aligned but generic, uneven, or inconsistent.
The tone is mostly aligned with only small mismatches.
The tone closely and consistently matches the requested tone throughout.

metric_definitions.definition: Measures whether the output works as a professional email.

rubric.description: The output is not usable as an email.
It barely resembles a professional email or collapses greeting, body, closing, and signature into a flat single paragraph.
Some email elements are present, but structure is incomplete, hard to scan, or mostly unformatted.
Basic email content is present, but paragraphing, greeting, body, or sign-off formatting is noticeably weak.
The email is clear and professional with only minor structure or formatting rough edges.
The subject and body are polished, clear, concise, and formatted with distinct greeting, body paragraphing, and sign-off.

metrics.reason: The email includes all required facts accurately: the May 17 meeting at 11:00 A.M., the attendees Kenneth Lay, Marty Sunde, and Bob Williams, and the request for exact instructions on where they should wait or go upon arrival. It is also concise, polite, and professional.

The email is concise, polite, and professional throughout. It clearly and respectfully requests the exact waiting instructions for the May 17 meeting attendees, includes the relevant names and time, and closes with a courteous thank-you and professional sign-off.

The email is clearly professional and easy to follow, with a distinct subject line, greeting, body, and closing. It communicates the request and includes the meeting date/time and attendee names. The only minor rough edge is that the body is formatted as a single paragraph rather than having more distinct paragraphing, so it is not fully polished to a 5.

The email includes all required facts: it schedules the project review meeting for next week, lists all three available slots (Tuesday 2-3 PM, Wednesday 10-11 AM, Thursday 1-2 PM), asks recipients to indicate their availability, and says the schedule needs to be finalized soon. It also correctly identifies the sender and affiliation.

The email is professional and concise, with a collaborative call for availability from the project team. It clearly asks for input on the proposed time slots and emphasizes finalizing the schedule soon, matching the requested tone throughout.

The email includes a clear subject line, a professional greeting, concise body content, and a proper sign-off with sender details. The main structure is strong, but the body is presented as a single block without distinct paragraphing, so it is polished but not fully optimal.

The email includes all required facts accurately: it references invoice 48217, states it was due on April 30, 2026, notes the outstanding amount of $3,450, and asks for an update on the expected payment date. It also matches the sender, recipient, and affiliation in a professional, polite tone.

The email is firm, polite, and professional throughout: it clearly states the overdue invoice details, requests an update on the expected payment date, and uses courteous language like "Could you please" and "Thank you for your prompt attention."

The email includes a clear subject line, a professional greeting, a complete body with the requested follow-up details, and a proper sign-off. However, the content is formatted as one continuous paragraph rather than using distinct body paragraphing and separated closing lines, so the structure has minor rough edges.

The email includes every required fact accurately: it states the original Tuesday 2:00 PM interview, explains there is a scheduling conflict, offers Wednesday 9:00 AM and Thursday 3:00 PM as alternatives, identifies the Software Engineer position at BrightPath Software Solutions, notes the 90-minute duration, and asks the candidate to let them know which time works. The tone is also professional, courteous, apologetic, and concise.

The email is professional, courteous, and apologetic throughout, with a concise request to reschedule and clear alternative times offered. It directly matches the requested tone with no noticeable mismatches.

The email includes a subject line, a clear greeting (“Dear Avery Johnson,”), a professional body that states the reason for rescheduling and offers two alternative times, and a courteous sign-off (“Best regards, Marcus Chen…”). However, the formatting is slightly rough because the greeting, body, and closing are presented in one continuous block rather than with distinct line breaks or clearly separated paragraphs.

The email includes all required facts: it identifies the North Shore Innovation Summit, states the conference dates as October 22-24, 2025, says early-bird registration ends on September 10, 2025, gives the $300 early-bird fee and $400 regular fee, and encourages recipients to register soon to secure the lower rate.

The email is informative and professional, clearly states the key dates and pricing, and uses an upbeat, encouraging close such as "We encourage you to register soon" and "We look forward to seeing you there!" The tone is consistent throughout and matches the requested informative, upbeat, professional style.

The email has all core parts of a professional message: a clear subject line, a greeting, body content, and a sign-off with affiliation. However, the body is written as one long block with no distinct paragraphing, so the formatting is noticeably weak rather than polished.

The email includes all required facts accurately: it says the design review is expected to be completed by Friday, 19 June 2026, explains the delay as due to missing stakeholder feedback, and states that no change to the final launch date is needed at this time.

The email clearly and calmly explains the delay, gives the revised completion date, reassures the recipient that the final launch date is unchanged, and thanks them for their patience. This closely matches the requested transparent, calm, and reassuring tone throughout.

The email has a clear subject line, a professional greeting, a coherent update, and a proper closing. The main structure is strong, but the body is presented as one continuous paragraph rather than using distinct body paragraphing, so it is polished but not fully ideal.

The email includes all required facts: it references the Q3 roadmap priorities discussion, says the attached notes summarize the key decisions and action items, and asks for corrections by the end of day Thursday. It also matches the sender, recipient, and professional tone without adding conflicting information.

The email is clear, organized, and professional throughout. It succinctly shares the meeting notes, states that they summarize key decisions and action items, and politely requests corrections by Thursday, matching the requested tone closely and consistently.

The email includes a clear subject line, a professional greeting, a coherent body, and a proper sign-off with name and title. The main structure is solid, but the body is presented as a single paragraph with limited paragraphing, so there are minor formatting rough edges.

The email includes all required facts accurately: it invites volunteers to a cleanup event on Saturday from 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM, asks them to bring gloves and wear closed-toe shoes, and says lunch and water will be provided. It also matches the friendly, appreciative tone and identifies the Community Outreach Organizer and Riverside Neighborhood Association.

The email is consistently friendly, encouraging, and appreciative: it warmly invites volunteers, expresses gratitude for their dedication, thanks them for their support, and uses a positive closing tone throughout.

The email includes a subject, greeting, body, closing, and signature, but they are all collapsed into one flat paragraph with no distinct paragraphing or line breaks, making it hard to scan and matching the rubric’s description of a minimally structured email.

The email includes all required facts: it states that visitors must check in at the front desk, says a government-issued photo ID is required for entry, and specifies that the new policy takes effect this coming Monday. The tone is formal and informative.

The email is formal and informative throughout, matching the requested tone closely. It uses polite, businesslike wording such as "Please be advised" and "Thank you for your cooperation," and clearly communicates the policy update with the required details.

The email includes a clear subject line, a formal greeting, a professional body, and a sign-off. It is easy to follow and matches the scenario, but the body is presented as a single paragraph rather than having distinct body paragraphing, so it is not fully polished for structure.

The email includes all required facts accurately: maintenance is scheduled for Sunday from 1:00 AM to 3:00 AM, the portal may be unavailable during that window, and service is expected to resume immediately after maintenance. It is also brief, reassuring, and professional.

The email is brief, professional, and reassuring throughout. It clearly states the maintenance window, notes the portal may be temporarily unavailable, and reassures users that service will resume immediately after maintenance without unnecessary detail.

The email has a clear subject and includes a greeting, body, and sign-off, but the content is presented as one largely unformatted block. The greeting, main message, and closing are not separated into distinct paragraphs or sections, making it harder to scan.
